publicationstmt.publisher: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2020-02-07 00:05:34 -0500.

change.item: This resource was modified by the ArchivesSpace Preprocessor developed by the Harvard Library (https://github.com/harvard-library/archivesspace-preprocessor)

repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.abstract: A collection of more than 150 individual, hand-drawn and printed street stickers, circa 2008-2011, the majority of which are unpeeled. The collection includes artwork by New York City street and sticker artists Kosby, Cost, Faust, Rednose, Robots Will Kill, 5003, Overconsumer, Snoe Man, Back Fat, Crasty, and many others. Also included are issues of the long out-of-print sticker zine "Carnage" (nos. 1, 2, and 4) as well as larger pieces of original artwork intended for wheatpasting. When seen on the streets, these stickers are usually weathered, faded, or torn. This collection presents an opportunity to study the art form in a vibrant, unpeeled condition.

defitem.label: Contact Information:
defitem.item: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524rareref@cornell.eduhttp://rmc.library.cornell.edu

bioghist.head: BIOGRAPHICAL NOTE
bioghist.p: Sticker art is a form of street art in which an image or message is publicly displayed using stickers. Artists frequently use cheap or free stickers as a basis for their art, such as those issued by the US Post Office, or name tags. Street art stickers can sometimes comprise a subcategory of graffiti.

scopecontent.head: COLLECTION DESCRIPTION
scopecontent.p: A collection of more than 150 individual, hand-drawn and printed street stickers, circa 2008-2011, the majority of which are unpeeled. The collection includes artwork by New York City street and sticker artists Kosby, Cost, Faust, Rednose, Robots Will Kill, 5003, Overconsumer, Snoe Man, Back Fat, Crasty, and many others. Also included are issues of the long out-of-print sticker zine "Carnage" (nos. 1, 2, and 4) as well as larger pieces of original artwork intended for wheatpasting. When seen on the streets, these stickers are usually weathered, faded, or torn. This collection presents an opportunity to study the art form in a vibrant, unpeeled condition.

arrangement.head: COLLECTION ARRANGEMENT
arrangement.p: Stickers are numbered 1-144 with the artist's name; some stickers contain multiple copies.

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Sticker Art Collection, #8121. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.
